data.title: Guoyuan Securities' 2024 Profit Jumps 20%
data.description:
Guoyuan Securities' attributable profit rose 20% to 2.24 billion yuan in 2024 from 1.87 billion yuan in 2023, according to an annual report filed Tuesday with the Shenzhen bourse.

Earnings per share at the brokerage increased 19% to 0.51 yuan from 0.43 yuan in the previous year.

Operating income grew 23% to 7.85 billion yuan from 6.36 billion yuan a year earlier.

data.title: Havells India to Invest INR6 Billion in Goldi Solar for Minority Stake
data.description:
Consumer durables company Havells India has agreed to invest 6 billion Indian rupees in solar modules and inverters manufacturer Goldi Solar, according to a Monday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The investment is part of funding round of up to 13 billion rupees by Goldi, which is expected to be completed within the next 75 days.

On completion of the transaction, Havells is estimated to have a shareholding of 8.90% to 9.24% in Goldi depending on the size of the total amount raised.

The deal is expected to be completed by June 30.

Havells already has a presence in the solar ecosystem through the sale of modules, inverters, solar cables, and DC switchgear. The company's investment in Goldi will help ensure a consistent supply of critical solar components like solar modules and cells.

data.title: Stocks gain on some tariff reprieve, currencies firm against soft dollar
data.description:
Key points:
  • The MSCI EM Asia index nudges higher
  • Singapore stocks up 1%, Indonesia rises 2%
  • Malaysia's ringgit at 7-week high
  • Singapore dollar hovers around early-November high

Asian emerging market equities climbed for a fourth consecutive session on Tuesday, supported by improved risk sentiment after U.S. President Donald Trump hinted at possible exemptions from auto-related tariffs.

Currencies drifted within narrow ranges against the dollar , which held near a two-year low versus a basket of major peers. Meanwhile, an MSCI index tracking emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) hovered at a seven-session high.

An index of stocks in Asian emerging markets (.MIMS00000PUS) rose marginally to a six-session high, while a gauge of equities in ASEAN countries (.MISU00000PUS) - dominated by Southeast Asian firms - scaled to a one-week high.

Cautious after recent bouts of volatile trading, investors extended the upbeat start this week as risk sentiment improved on signs of potential tariff exemptions for certain sectors and optimism that punitive levies might be scaled back through negotiations.

Overnight, Trump said he was considering a modification to the 25% tariffs imposed on foreign auto and auto parts imports, sending shares of carmakers such as Toyota , Hyundai Motor and its sister firm Kia Corp soaring.

Earlier, the White House exempted certain electronic items from steep tariffs on imports from China but later announced a probe into imports of semiconductors. Trump had earlier warned levies on the sector were likely.

"At this juncture, tariff narrative has turned more constructive, as opposed to chaotic in the last week," analysts at DBS wrote in a note.

"A more organised tariff framework around pharmaceuticals, semiconductors and autos is probably in the works. With more time to adjust supply chains, potential disruptions could be minimised."

South Korea's KOSPI index advanced around 1%, led by the automakers, while Taiwan's benchmark TWSE:TAIEX added 1.5%. Contract chipmaker TSMC rebounded 1.5% after a sharp drop the previous day.

In Southeast Asia, Indonesia's benchmark soared almost 2% to scale a near three-week high, marking their fourth consecutive session of gains.

Singapore's FTSE Straits Times index advanced more than 1%, consolidating after a sharp 12% decline over the prior two weeks.

Stocks in Malaysia and the Philippines were largely unchanged.

Among regional currencies, Singapore's dollar edged higher, hovering around a five-month high of 1.3144 per U.S. dollar. Malaysia's ringgit also appreciated as much as 4.3970 a dollar for the first time in seven weeks.

The Philippine peso and the Thai baht also nudged higher, with the latter also touching its best level in more than seven weeks.

Indonesia's rupiah slipped marginally to 16,810 per U.S. dollar after three sessions of gains.

"Emerging market currencies can get some pressure from a weaker risk sentiment, although dollar weakness can offset some of the negative impact from this," Maybank analysts said.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** Yields on Indonesia's 10-year benchmark bonds (ID10YT=RR) creep lower to 7.007%

** China, Vietnam sign deals as Xi visits Hanoi amid U.S. tariff tensions

** Thailand says to discuss tariffs with the United States on April 21

** Indonesia, Qatar to create $4 bln joint fund investing in Indonesia

data.title: What could knock King Dollar off its throne: podcast
data.description:
Follow on Apple or Spotify. Listen on the Reuters app. Read the episode transcript.

Listen to the podcast

The world’s dominant currency has survived devaluation, inflation, war, and many crises. US President Donald Trump’s return raises new doubts. In this episode of The Big View podcast, Paul Blustein talks about the secrets of the greenback’s success – and what could end its reign.

Follow @peter_tl on X

FURTHER READING/LISTENING

King Dollar’s long reign is set to continue

Can the dollar remain king of currencies? – Barry Eichengreen in the Financial Times

A User’s Guide to Restructuring the Global Trading System – Stephen Miran

Why financial warfare could backfire on the US: podcast

The power and peril of American economic warfare: podcast

Visit the Thomson Reuters Privacy Statement for information on our privacy and data protection practices. You may also visit megaphone.fm/adchoices to opt-out of targeted advertising.

data.title: African Markets - Factors to watch on April 15
data.description:
The following company announcements, scheduled economic indicators, debt and currency market moves and political events may affect African markets on Tuesday.

- - - - -

EVENTS:

*Nigeria's statistics office is due to release its consumer inflation data for March.

GLOBAL MARKETS

Asia shares edged slightly higher on Tuesday, helped by gains in auto companies after U.S. President Donald Trump suggested he might grant exemptions on auto-related levies already in place.

WORLD OIL PRICES

Oil prices inched higher on Tuesday, supported by new tariff exemptions floated by U.S. President Donald Trump and a rebound in China crude oil imports in anticipation of tighter Iranian supply.

EMERGING MARKETS

For the top emerging markets news, double click on

AFRICA STOCKS

For the latest news on South African stocks, click on

SOUTH AFRICA MARKETS

The South African rand firmed on Monday, boosted by a news report that the country's biggest political party may back down on a plan to raise value-added tax (VAT) and dollar weakness on global markets.

NIGERIA PETROLEUM

Nigeria's Dangote oil refinery has issued a tender to sell 80,000-88,000 metric tons of reduced crude oil (RCO), an atmospheric residue similar to fuel oil, a document seen by Reuters showed.

KENYA MARKETS

Kenya's shilling was slightly stronger on Monday, though traders said they was expected it to weaken soon because of increased dollar demand from manufacturers and banks covering short positions.

GHANA GOLD

Ghana has ordered foreigners to exit its gold trading market by the end of the month, a new government body said on Monday, as the West African country looks to streamline gold purchases from small-scale miners, increase earnings and reduce smuggling.

IVORY COAST COCOA

Cocoa arrivals at ports in top grower Ivory Coast since the start of the season on October 1 had reached 1.457 million metric tons by April 13, up from 1.317 million tons from the same period last season, exporters estimated on Monday.

data.title: Resources & Energy to Sell Queensland Project to QMines; Shares Jump 19%
data.description:
Resources & Energy Group is set to sell its Mount Mackenzie gold project in Queensland to QMines for AU$2.5 million, according to a Tuesday filing with the Australian bourse.

The consideration comprises AU$1 million in cash and the issues at 33 million QML shares at AU$0.045 each, the filing said.

Completion is subject to shareholder approval, due diligence, and tenement standing, with settlement expected within 10 business days upon meeting conditions, the filing added.

Shares of Resources & Energy jumped 19% in recent Tuesday trade.

data.title: Australia, NZ dollars extend winning streak as greenback loses lustre
data.description:
The Australian and New Zealand dollars were trying for a fifth straight session of gains on Tuesday as their U.S. counterpart struggled to sustain its safe-haven status, while a break of key chart levels triggered stop-loss demand.

The turnaround for the Antipodeans has been remarkable, with both rallying almost 7% since hitting five-year lows a week ago.

The Aussie has made it back to $0.6353 , up from a $0.5910 trough and around where it was when President Donald Trump stunned the markets with the announcement of his "reciprocal" tariffs.

The kiwi dollar hit its highest in more than four months at $0.5914 , cracking resistance at $0.5852 and testing the next barrier at $0.5930.

The recovery owed much to fears the ever-changing tariffs would hobble U.S. growth and shake confidence in its assets.

"We see the tariff war as primarily an act of self-harm that will leave the U.S. teetering on the edge of recession," said Luci Ellis, head of economics at Westpac.

"While many asset managers we speak to see no alternative to the Treasury market for its size and liquidity, at least some wish there was one," she added. "Given the USD was already well above estimates of fair value, these investors would also prefer their portfolios to be less concentrated in USD."

Minutes of the Reserve Bank of Australia's April policy meeting out on Tuesday showed tariffs dominated the discussion and the board hinted it would be open to easing again at its next meeting on May 19/20.

Markets are fully priced for a quarter-point cut in the 4.10% cash rate, and imply around a 30% chance it may move by 50 basis points. (0#AUDIRPR)

The Reserve Bank of New Zealand cut its rates by a quarter point to 3.5% last week and investors are pricing further easing to 2.75% by the autumn. (0#NZDIRPR)

One hurdle will be consumer price data due on Thursday, which is forecast to show inflation nudged up to 2.3% in the first quarter, from 2.2% the previous quarter.

Some of the partial figures for March suggest there's a risk of a higher outcome, though not enough to truly challenge the market's dovish view.

data.title: Dongshan Precision Manufacturing Expects Up to 60% Rise in Q1 Profit; Shares Down 3%
data.description:
Suzhou Dongshan Precision Manufacturing forecasted first-quarter net profit attributable to shareholders to range between 434.0 million yuan and 463.0 million yuan.

This marked an increase of between 50% and 60% from the year-ago profit of 289.3 million yuan, according to a Tuesday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

Earnings per share are forecasted to rise to between 0.26 yuan and 0.27 yuan from 0.17 yuan.

The precision metals manufacturer's shares were down 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Palm Oil Extend Declines
data.description:
Malaysian palm oil futures fell over 1.5% to below MYR 4,100 per tonne, marking a second straight session of steep losses amid mounting worries that prices may stay under pressure in the coming months due to lingering global economic uncertainty.

While a three-month pause in U.S. tariffs offers temporary relief and gives buyers time to stock up, the oleochemical sector—including palm oil—could face renewed headwinds once the reprieve ends.

Meanwhile, caution lingered ahead of China’s Q1 2025 GDP readings later this week as markets braced for a slowdown due to multiple challenges, particularly in trade.

Still, losses were limited by cargo surveyor data showing Malaysian palm oil exports surged 29.3% to 52.8% month-on-month in the first ten days of April.

In India, March palm oil imports rose almost 14%, with further gains likely as inventories run low.

Meanwhile, Kuala Lumpur is reportedly working to expand exports to South Africa and the Middle East in response to U.S. tariff concerns.

data.title: Correction to RBI Article on April 9
data.description: The Reserve Bank of India changed its monetary-policy stance to accommodative from neutral. "India's Central Bank Cuts Key Rate, Signals More Support for Economy — Update," at 0742 GMT on April 9, incorrectly said it was to neutral from accommodative.

data.title: ZA FUNDINGS LTD Strengthens Security for Cloud Mining with Cold Wallet Storage and Enterprise-Grade Protections
data.description:
ZA FUNDINGS LTD Enhances Cloud Mining Security with Cold Wallet Storage and Advanced Enterprise Protections

BERLIN, April 15, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — ZA FUNDINGS LTD, a leading provider of cloud mining services, has announced a major upgrade to its security infrastructure to better protect user funds and ensure the integrity of its cloud mining operations. With the increasing cyber risks in the digital asset sector, the company has implemented a multi-layered defense strategy that includes offline cold wallet storage, McAfee® SECURE systems, and Cloudflare® integration.

Comprehensive Security Measures for Cloud Mining Funds

As part of its commitment to secure and transparent cloud mining services, ZA FUNDINGS has adopted stringent security protocols. A critical enhancement involves storing the majority of user funds in offline cold wallets, which are physically disconnected from the network, eliminating exposure to online threats. This approach significantly reduces the risk of large-scale breaches and protects the integrity of cloud mining earnings.

The cold wallet storage system is designed with redundancy and manual verification steps to ensure both reliability and controlled access. In addition, McAfee® SECURE protection actively monitors the platform, identifying and neutralizing potential malware, vulnerabilities, and suspicious activity before they impact cloud mining services.

Cloudflare® Integration for Enhanced Web Security

To further enhance the security of its cloud mining platform, ZA FUNDINGS has integrated Cloudflare® solutions to protect against DDoS attacks, bot activity, and other external threats. This integration ensures minimal downtime and uninterrupted access to cloud mining services, allowing users to confidently engage in mining activities across various regions.

Ongoing Commitment to Compliance and Cybersecurity

ZA FUNDINGS’s security upgrades reflect its dedication to operating within the highest international standards. The company is certified by the UK’s Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), ensuring that its cloud mining operations comply with stringent data protection and operational integrity requirements. The company also conducts regular third-party audits and internal security reviews to ensure continuous improvement of all protective systems.

Looking to the future, ZA FUNDINGS plans to expand its threat detection capabilities and partner with additional cybersecurity experts to safeguard its cloud mining operations against evolving digital threats.

Media Contact:

Company: ZA FUNDINGS LTD

Contact Person: Anisah Fatema Sheikh

Email: info@zaminer

Website: https://www.zaminer.com/

Photos accompanying this announcement are available at

https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/2dc579c0-2657-48be-b111-a43587683333

https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/cc105d86-daa4-4e7c-a36e-3e07bfb36771

data.title: India's Macrotech jumps after CEO, kin settle brand dispute
data.description:
** Shares of India's Macrotech Developers , formerly Lodha Developers, rise 3.6% to 1,163 rupees

** CEO and MD Abhishek Lodha and his brother Abhinandan Lodha settle trademark dispute of "Lodha" branding, multiple local media outlets report over the weekend

** Both sides agree that Abhishek's Macrotech to own "Lodha" brand, while brother Abhinandan to own "House of Abhinandan Lodha", reports say

** On the day, Nifty Realty index rises 3%, among top sectoral winners by pct amid broad based market gains

** YTD, MACE has fallen 17%, Nifty Realty has lost over 22%

data.title: Wheat slips on forecasts for rain in US cropping regions
data.description:
Chicago wheat futures edged lower for a second day on Tuesday after weather forecasts predicted better growing conditions in the U.S. wheat belt and prices fell in Europe and Russia.

Corn futures rose slightly and soybeans fell but both remained close to Monday's seven-week highs.

All three crops were supported by a weaker dollar, making U.S. farm goods cheaper for overseas buyers. The U.S. dollar index is sitting near three-year lows after U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff policies shook global markets.

The most-active wheat contract on the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) was down 0.1% at $5.46-3/4 a bushel at 0353 GMT after reaching $5.56-3/4 on Friday, its highest level since March 24.

CBOT soybeans for May delivery fell 0.5% to $10.36-3/4 a bushel, near Monday's peak of $10.49-1/2, while July corn was 0.2% higher at $4.93-1/2 a bushel, having reached $4.97-1/2 on Monday.

Weather forecasts predicted much-needed rain next week in the U.S. Plains, as well as in cropping regions in France and Germany.

Around 32% of the U.S. winter wheat crop was affected by drought as of April 8, the U.S. Department of Agriculture said last week, up from 15% a year ago.

The USDA in a weekly crop progress report on Monday rated 47% of the U.S. winter wheat crop in good to excellent condition, down from 48% a week earlier.

May wheat (BL2K5) on the Euronext exchange fell on Monday to its lowest in a year, and Russian wheat export prices edged lower last week as weather for the new crop improved, according to analysts.

"We're expecting prices to move higher over the coming year," said Commonwealth Bank analyst Dennis Voznesenski, adding that price support should come from lower ending stocks in key wheat exporting countries.

U.S. crop shipments are holding up well, with the USDA reporting weekly export inspections of wheat and corn that exceeded analyst expectations.

Traders say top global soy buyer China seems to be closed to U.S. grain and soybeans for the foreseeable future but they hope negotiations can open U.S. sales to other markets.

data.title: BioPorto Starts Private Placement to Fund Clinical Trials
data.description:
BioPorto said Monday it plans to raise 33.5 million Danish kroner from a private placement of up to 25 million new shares priced at 1.3402 apiece.

The subscription period started immediately and will close at short notice, with results expected on Monday and trading on Nasdaq Copenhagen expected no later than April 30.

The Danish in-vitro diagnostics company will use the net proceeds to fund clinical trials for the US Food and Drug Administration clearance of ProNephro AKI (NGAL) in adults, and to enhance sales and marketing for its current product line, and support general corporate purposes.

BioPorto said the placement is the next phase in its financing strategy to secure 55 million kroner in the first half.

data.title: Macrotech Developers Retains 'Lodha' Brand as Brothers Resolve Disputes; Shares Climb 3%
data.description:
Macrotech Developers said the Lodha brothers - Abhishek Lodha and Abhinandan Lodha - have resolved all outstanding disputes, including those related to trademark, according to a Monday Indian bourse filing.

Macrotech Developers will have exclusive rights to the 'Lodha' and 'Lodha Group' brand names, while Abhinandan Lodha will have exclusive rights to use the 'House of Abhinandan Lodha' brand, the filing said.

The two brand entities are unrelated, and neither brother has any claim over the other's businesses, it added.

Shares of Macrotech Developers were up 2.7% in recent trading.

data.title: CNLight's Building Under Investigation After Fire
data.description:
Authorities are investigating a fire that broke out in a building owned by Chinese lamp maker CNLight , according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange filing on Sunday.

The building had been leased to an external tenant, a cosmetics company.

The company said the incident had no casualties and its operations remain normal, but it will initiate a comprehensive safety inspection across all factory areas to prevent similar incidents, the filing said.

The company's shares fell over 2% in recent trade.

data.title: Scandinavian Enviro Systems Plans SEK298 Million Unit Rights Issue
data.description:
Scandinavian Enviro Systems said Monday its board aims to raise 297.6 million kronor by issuing new units with preferential rights to existing shareholders.

Shareholders on the record as of May 16 will receive one unit right for each share held, with nine unit rights entitling a subscription for one unit. Each unit comprises four newly issued shares and one new warrant.

The Swedish recycling company said it has received subscription intentions, subscription undertakings, and guarantee commitments for a total of 240.1 million kronor, or 80.7% of the rights issue.

Enviro intends to use the net proceeds to repay its bridge loan, finance the construction of a plant in Uddevalla, Sweden, fund other initiatives, and for general corporate purposes.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Financial Services News of the Day - Tuesday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Wall Street's Trading Revenue Powered by Tariff Uncertainty

Goldman Sachs is the latest big bank to report that increased trading fueled its quarterly profit.

The U.S. Corporate Bond Market Needs a Boost. Big Banks Are Helping.

Just over $15 billion worth of investment-grade bonds have been issued in April, the slowest start to the month since 2009.

Bank Trading Desks Are Minting Money From Trump's Tariff Chaos

Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley all saw huge jumps in trading revenue.

CME Group, S&P Group Agree to Sell OSTTRA to KKR in $3.1 Billion Deal

CME Group and S&P Global have agreed to sell OSTTRA to investment funds managed by investment firm KKR for an enterprise value of $3.1 billion.

Morgan Stanley Collects $2.3 Billion for Private Equity Co-Investments

The investment bank's asset management arm sees robust demand for co-investment capital, particularly to co-underwrite deals.

BNP Paribas Downgrades Projections for AXA Investment Managers Deal

The lender expects a higher capital impact and lower return on investment than initially projected for its acquisition of AXA Investment Managers, after the ECB clarified its regulatory stance.

MPS Gets Italy's Green Light for Mediobanca Deal as Top Shareholders Signal Support

Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena launched a surprise $14 billion-takeover offer for its peer in January.

Temasek-Backed Private-Equity Firm Raises Over $600 Mln in Latest Funding Round

Private-equity firm ABC Impact has raised over $600.0 million in its latest funding round, drawing interest from investors, including a multilateral development bank and a sovereign-wealth fund.

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Ashmore, Goldman Sachs and more in the latest Market Talks covering Financial Services.

7 Market Pros on Wall Street's Wild Month

President Trump's tariff policy has set off a wave of selling in the stock and bond markets. These savvy investors are buying, too.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Tuesday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Europe's Appetite for Wind Power Helps Sandbrook Capital Find an Exit

Sandbrook Capital scored its first exit, a roughly $1 billion deal for a Norwegian service provider for offshore-wind-energy project developers, benefitting from the sector's fast expansion in Europe even as it fell into disfavor in the U.S.

Oil Futures Edge Up in Choppy Session

Crude futures rose in an up-and-down session, with U.S. tariff relief on electronic goods helping to push up prices. WTI settled up 3 cents at $61.53 a barrel, and Brent rose 0.2% to $64.88 a barrel.

OPEC Cuts Oil Demand Outlook Amid U.S. Tariff Hit

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries cut its forecast for oil demand growth just days after a surprise decision to boost output, citing the impact of U.S. tariffs on the global economy and crude consumption.

BP Discovers Oil at U.S. Far South Prospect in Gulf of Mexico

Initial data shows high-quality reservoirs and a potentially commercial volume of hydrocarbons, the company said.

Trump Has a $350 Billion Deal for Europe: Buy Our Energy

The continent has sharply increased its U.S. purchases in recent years, with little appetite to buy more.

Nuclear Power Is Back. And This Time, AI Can Help Manage the Reactors.

Argonne National Lab has an AI-based tool that can help design and operate nuclear reactors-at a time when AI itself is feeding a power frenzy.

BP Expects First-Quarter Upstream Production to Fall

The company anticipates lower upstream production than the previous quarter due to already announced divestments in Egypt and Trinidad.

Trump Promised a Golden Age for Oil and Gas, but Tariffs Undermine His Pledge

Fractured trade relations and a potential global recession are poised to reduce international sales and domestic output of fossil fuels.

AI Boom to Fuel Surge in Data Center Energy Needs, IEA Says

The global push for artificial intelligence is poised to fuel a sharp rise in electricity demand, with consumption from data centers expected to more than double by the end of the decade, according to the International Energy Agency.

Equinor Creates New Power Business to Cater to Data-Center Demand

The company said growing demand for power as well as the expansion of AI and data centers drove the move.

data.title: KINGMAKERS OF OAKLAND EXPANDS NATIONAL ACCESS IN CAPACITY BUILDING WITH CROWNS CURRICULUM AND INTRODUCES JEGNA EARLY LITERACY PROGRAM
data.description:
Oakland, CA, April 15, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Oakland, CA – April 14, 2025 – Kingmakers of Oakland (KOO), a leading organization dedicated to transforming educational outcomes for African-American boys, announces national access to its two highly impactful school-based programs: the Crowns Curriculum and Jegna Early Literacy. These programs are designed to empower African-American boys and foster educational environments that support their academic, emotional, and leadership growth.

The Crowns Curriculum is a revolutionary, wrap-around approach that highlights the beauty, brilliance, and innate greatness of African-American boys. It is rooted in asset-based pedagogy, which focuses on the strengths and potential of students, instead of their deficits. The curriculum aims to create learning spaces that foster emotional safety, belonging, and a collective sense of responsibility. With an emphasis on cultural identity and holistic development, the Crowns Curriculum prepares students for leadership roles and empowers them to question systems of oppression while designing African-centered alternatives to the status quo.

Kingmakers of Oakland also introduces the expansion of the Jegna Early Literacy program. The word, Jegna, originates from the ancient African kingdom of Abyssinia, and is a title of honor that encompasses meanings such as hero, warrior, courage, strength, and the protection of culture, land, and people. The Jegna Early Literacy Program is a cohort model that has been shown to be an effective strategy for achieving literacy goals and objectives. Jegna facilitators will work in classrooms two to three days a week, coming together weekly for training, support, and to share success stories, solve problems, and encourage one another creating a pipeline of male educators.

The Jegna Early Literacy program is designed for students in TK-3rd grade, and includes a series of research-based books and materials for training and use in schools. The program has a strong emphasis on Belonging, Self-Efficacy, Sense of Community, and Cultural Identity—four key pillars that have been shown to support the success of African-American children. Jegna facilitators will also receive ongoing professional development in early literacy and, if available, training opportunities will be extended to teachers in the classrooms where Jegna facilitators are placed.  Two Jegna facilitators are currently assigned in Oakland and have recently experienced an unprecedented opportunity to travel to Panama on a literacy initiative through the program demonstrating the need and potential reach within the African diaspora.

Kingmakers of Oakland is committed to expanding these evidence-based programs to schools and districts nationwide, aiming to cultivate environments where African-American boys feel emotionally safe, supported, and capable of achieving academic success, while fostering cultural awareness and leadership.For more information about the Crowns Curriculum, the Jegna Early Literacy Program, or to inquire about bringing these programs to your community, please contact matin@kingmakersofoaokland.org.

 About Kingmakers of Oakland  

Kingmakers of Oakland reimagines systems of education, culture, and wellness while driving narrative change that celebrates African-American culture and improves educational and life outcomes for African-American boys from Pre-K to Ph.D. The organization’s mission is to empower these young men, helping them to see themselves as brilliant, beautiful, and capable of reaching their full potential. By working in solidarity with other organizations and engaging in transformative dialogue, Kingmakers of Oakland is committed to creating opportunities and advancing equity for African-American boys in education and beyond.  

Chris Chatmon

Kingmakers of Oakland

chris@kingmakersofoakland.org

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0321 GMT - UBS trims another 10% off its 2026 price forecast for lithium-rich spodumene, now projecting an average of US$763 a metric ton for next year versus spot price of US$770. It also pares its long-term price forecast by 7% to US$1,300. The bank's Australian analysts say that while the beleaguered lithium market is probably past its nadir, the battery ingredient is headed for a gradual recovery. The analysts pare their stock-price target on lithium producer Pilbara Minerals to A$1.50 from A$2.00. They upgrade Pilbara Minerals to a neutral from sell but cite valuation grounds. Pilbara Minerals is down 1.8% at A$1.39. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0253 GMT - Iron ore prices are higher in early Asian trade. Steel mills are actively operating during the peak season, and the consumption of iron ore continues to pick up, Baocheng Futures says in a note. The average daily molten iron output and the use of imported iron ore at sample steel mills both increased on month last week, the brokerage says in a note. However, iron ore prices will likely fluctuate going forward given ongoing tariff issues, it says. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.2% higher at CNY707.5 a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0246 GMT - Palm oil prices fall in Asian trade, tracking weakness in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. Palm olein's softness on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is also weighing on palm oil prices, he says. Slower exports pace and expectation of rising production could further pressure palm oil prices, he adds. Ng sees support for crude palm oil prices at 4,050 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,300 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia derivatives contract for June delivery is down 56 ringgit at 4,114 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0125 GMT - Base metals are broadly higher in early Asian trade. Risk on-sentiment is improving due to reduced trade uncertainty for now, though the lack of a strong upward momentum in prices shows that traders remain cautious, analysts at Sucden Financial writes in a note. The three-month LME copper contract is up 0.3% at $9,212.00/ton, while aluminum and zinc up by 0.5% and 0.2% respectively.(kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0109 GMT - Malaysia's plantation sector may face headwinds ahead, says AmInvestment Bank analyst Gan Huey Ling in a note. While the three-month pause in U.S. tariffs gives customers time to stock up, the oleochemical industry could face challenges once the reprieve ends, she says. These include weaker global demand, rising competition from Indonesia and China, oversupply and margin pressure, she reckons. Still, palm oil demand could find support from China's reduced reliance on U.S. soybeans and a potential increase in the U.S. biodiesel mandate, she adds. However, Gan thinks selling prices for oleochemicals may remain under pressure in 2H due to lingering economic uncertainties. AmInvestment downgrades Malaysia's plantation sector to neutral from overweight, but maintains buy ratings on Genting Plantations, Johor Plantations, and TSH Resources. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2340 GMT - Gold edges higher in early morning Asian session as traders digest mixed signals. On the one hand, Fed governor Waller said overnight that rates may have to be lowered soon if President Trump's large-sized tariffs remain in place, supporting the appeal of the non-interest-bearing precious metal. On the other hand, Trump said Monday that he's looking at pausing some tariffs to help auto companies, which may damp the safe-haven allure of gold. "In the near-term gold is once again looking a touch stretched and a period of consolidation may be warranted," says Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at City Index and FOREX.com, in an email. Spot gold is 0.1% higher at $3,212.53/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1933 GMT - Gold futures fall from lofty levels, slipping 0.5% to $3,204.80 per troy ounce, still the second-highest ever close for the front-month contract. The decline is not expected to become a short-term trend, says Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities USA in a note. "Tariff headlines continue to hit the tape, the dollar continues to slide, and the Chinese continue to lurk in the shadows while holding the second most amount of U.S. debt on the planet," Yawger says. "That should allow gold to attract enough safe haven flow to at least consolidate within striking distance of the all-time record high." (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1916 GMT - Crude futures eke out modest gains in an up-and-down session, with U.S. tariff relief on electronic goods helping and a 150,000 barrels-a-day cut in OPEC's demand growth outlook weighing on prices. "The diverging picture between inventories and global demand may drive volatility in crude oil markets for some time," Kenny Zhu of Global X says in a note. "This is keenly exhibited by the disconnect between near-term supply/demand dynamics and expected demand." WTI settles up 3 cents at $61.53 a barrel, and Brent rises 0.2% to $64.88 a barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1911 GMT - Lean hog futures on the CME rise 1.9% to 95.375 cents a pound. This makes it four consecutive sessions that hog futures close higher, with prices rebounding after President Trump paused reciprocal tariffs on numerous nations, with tariffs remaining on China. "The hog market is recovering from last week's extreme over-reaction to China tariff news," said Rich Nelson of Allendale Inc. "These days China is only 6.5% of our exports, the equivalent of 1.7% of US pork production." Live cattle futures also rise, closing up 1.2% to $1.9925 a pound. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1855 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures fall for a third straight session as the weather turns and with it demand patterns from a predominance of heating to less gas-intensive cooling. "The pattern for late April into May favors light demand as heating degree days slowly fade as the northern U.S. warms, while cooling degree days gradually increase as highs of 80s and 90s gain ground across the southern and central U.S.," NatGasWeather.com says in a note. On the bullish side is still high LNG feedgas demand, while stronger year-over-year wind and solar power generation is bearish for gas, the forecaster adds. "And with strong winds across many regions, renewables will be quite strong this week." Nymex natural gas settles down 5.7% at $3.325/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1835 GMT - The planting progress of U.S. farmers in the past week is a key metric grain traders are looking for, which the USDA will be reporting in its Crop Progress report this afternoon. Some 6% of the projected corn crop and 3% of the soybean crops are expected to have been planted nationally, according to AgResource in a note. The firm adds that it sees a good chance for the USDA to report planting to be even higher in this week's report. AgResource also notes that it expects the USDA to report reduced quality of U.S. wheat crops, amid weather introducing flooding to many crop-growing areas. Most-active CBOT corn is down 1.2% late in trading, soybeans fall 0.1%, and wheat falls 1.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1822 GMT - Oil prices appear to have stabilized following the U.S. "Liberation Day" tariff rollout, but at levels that underscore the market's bearish view on demand, John Coleman of Sparta Commodities says in a note. "Trade war frictions continue to weigh on WTI, as retaliatory tariffs, and likely more policy uncertainty to come, force it to price far more competitively than usual," he says. WTI last week appeared to offer the best relative value globally, but the U.S. benchmark light crude lost some of its competitive edge with a softening in North Sea prices and a "near-collapse of Middle Eastern premia," he adds. WTI is off 0.6% at $61.13 a barrel, and Brent is off 0.4% at $64.52 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Uncertainty Prevails Amid Hopes for Tariff Exemptions
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

EU industrial production; U.K. unemployment; Germany ZEW indicator of economic sentiment; trading updates from Ericsson, Publicis Groupe, Beiersdorf

Opening Call:

European stock futures were tracking mixed in tandem with mixed trading in Asian equities. The dollar gained slightly while U.S. Treasury yields were lower. Oil futures were little changed, and gold gained.

Equities:

European stock futures were mixed early Tuesday amid tentative hopes for more U.S. tariff exemptions that could lessen the negative impact of tougher trade barriers.

U.S. stocks overcame a midday swoon to end higher on Monday after optimism about President Trump's tariff exemptions spread from technology goods to America's car industry.

Investors took some relief from the latest U.S. tariff exemptions on consumer electronics, even though separate tariffs for the sector still loom, Commerzbank Research team said.

In the weeks ahead, investors will parse corporate earnings reports for clues on how the largest American companies are preparing for-or already feeling the impact of-Trump's tariff plans. But even strong profits might not be enough to shift the tone of the stock market after so much whipsawing on policy.

"The reality is now there will be no relief to the uncertainty," Ryan said. "I think this is our world for the foreseeable future," said Dann Ryan, a managing partner at Sincerus Advisory.

Forex:

The U.S. dollar edged higher as traders weighed mixed signals on U.S. tariffs. "After announcing a temporary reprieve on the electronics tariffs, President Trump said he is exploring possible temporary exemptions to his tariffs on imported cars and parts to give car makers more time to set up U.S. factories," CBA's Carol Kong said.

However, "tariffs on imports of semiconductors and pharmaceuticals are still in the pipeline." Uncertainty over U.S. policy-making and the global economic outlook is still high, Kong added.

Bonds:

The worst of the Treasury selloff is likely over, said Harbor Capital's Jake Schurmeier, "absent another big shock." The 10-year yield peaked near 4.5% last Friday and has since declined following a step back on some tariffs.

"What you are going to see is a higher risk premium on U.S. assets," Schurmeier says. "In the Treasurys space that is going to be a higher term premium and in the equity space lower multiples." Schurmeier sees the dwindling demand for U.S. government debt as a long-term trend.

Energy:

Oil futures were little changed after this morning's highs as demand concerns remain intact, with OPEC cutting its growth outlook for this year by 150,000 barrels a day. Oil price gains reflect the decline in Treasury yields and a weaker U.S. dollar that's positive for European buyers, Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital said.

"However, we do not expect prices to rise significantly above this morning's high levels," he added.

Metals:

Gold edged higher as traders digested mixed signals. On the one hand, Fed Gov. Waller said overnight that rates may have to be lowered soon if President Trump's large-sized tariffs remain in place, supporting the appeal of the non-interest-bearing precious metal.

On the other hand, Trump said Monday that he's looking at pausing some tariffs to help auto companies, which may damp the safe-haven allure of gold.

"In the near-term gold is once again looking a touch stretched and a period of consolidation may be warranted," said Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at City Index and FOREX.com.

--

Base metals were broadly higher. Risk on-sentiment was improving due to reduced trade uncertainty for now, though the lack of a strong upward momentum in prices showed that traders remained cautious, analysts at Sucden Financial said.

--

Iron ore prices were higher. Steel mills are actively operating during the peak season, and the consumption of iron ore continues to pick up, Baocheng Futures said. However, iron ore prices will likely fluctuate going forward given ongoing tariff issues, it said.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Fed's Waller says interest rates may need to be cut soon if largest Trump tariffs stay in place

If President Donald Trump's large tariffs remain in place for some time, the economy is likely to slow to a crawl, Federal Reserve governor Chris Waller said Monday. As a result, interest-rate cuts would be warranted, even if the taxes on imports cause a spike in inflation.

"If the slowdown is significant and even threatens a recession, then I would expect to favor cutting the [Federal Open Market Committee's] policy rate sooner, and to a greater extent than I had previously thought," Waller said in a speech to the Chartered Financial Analyst Society St. Louis.

The U.S. Corporate Bond Market Needs a Boost. Big Banks Are Helping.

U.S. firms have been holding back from raising debt in April, but the market is now getting a boost from big banks, which typically sell debt after earnings reports.

Just over $15 billion worth of investment-grade bonds, or higher rated debt, had been issued in April as of Friday, the slowest start to the month since 2009, according to BMO Capital Markets. Wall Street's expectation for this month is $105 billion this month.

Aides Push Trump to Adopt Tougher Approach With Moscow

WASHINGTON-Several senior aides to President Trump are advising him to be more skeptical of Moscow's desire for peace with Ukraine, arguing that Russian President Vladimir Putin has yet to show genuine interest in halting the fighting.

The group, which U.S. officials said includes Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Ukraine envoy Keith Kellogg, has recommended more caution when dealing with Putin and for a harder line against Moscow's demands for territorial concessions from Kyiv.

Luxury Empire LVMH Posts Revenue Below Views Amid Global Trade War Threat

Louis Vuitton owner LVMH posted sales below analysts' forecasts for the first quarter as a slowdown that has pervaded the luxury-goods industry for months lingers on.

The conglomerate owned by French billionaire Bernard Arnault reported revenue of 20.31 billion euros ($23.08 billion) for the first three months of the year, down 3% organically compared to the prior-year period.

Bank Trading Desks Are Minting Money From Trump's Tariff Chaos

Wall Street is raking in even more money from trading than it did during the market's Covid-19 era swings.

Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan Chase and Morgan Stanley all said a surge in trading revenue helped lift their profits in the first quarter and beat expectations. Bank trading desks have been collecting more fees from investors scrambling to reduce or dial up the risk in their portfolios on any new clue about how President Trump's tariffs might ultimately play out.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Tuesday

06:00/ROM: Feb Industrial production

06:00/NOR: Mar External trade in goods

06:00/GER: Mar WPI

06:00/UK: Mar UK monthly unemployment figures

06:45/FRA: Mar CPI

07:00/SVK: Mar CPI

07:00/SVK: Mar Core & net inflation development

08:00/FRA: Apr IEA Oil Market Report

08:00/POL: Mar CPI

09:00/EU: Feb Industrial Production

09:00/CRO: Mar CPI

09:05/GER: Apr ZEW Indicator of Economic Sentiment

10:00/IRL: Feb Goods Exports and Imports

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Graphano Receives Exploration Work Authorisations for all Quebec Properties
data.description:
Vancouver, British Columbia--(Newsfile Corp. - April 15, 2025) - Graphano Energy Ltd. (OTC Pink: GELEF) (FSE: 97G0) ("Graphano" or the "Company") is pleased to announce that it has received "Autorisation de Travaux à Impact" ("ATI"), the authorization of impact-causing exploration work from the Ministère des Ressources naturelles et des Forêts ("MRNF") for all its Québec-based properties: Lac Aux Bouleaux, Standard Mine, and Pearl Noir (Black Pearl).

"We are pleased to have secured all necessary ATI approvals for our Québec properties", said Dr. Luisa Moreno, CEO of Graphano. "This milestone reflects our commitment to responsible exploration and adherence to Québec's regulatory framework. With these authorizations in hand, we are well-positioned to advance our exploration initiatives and unlock the potential of our high-quality graphite assets."​

These ATI approvals encompass all designated exploration zones and newly identified discovery targets across the three properties, enabling the Company to proceed with its 2025 field programs.

Government Authorizations for Exploration

In May 2024, Québec introduced new exploration permitting requirements to enhance transparency and incorporate community feedback into mineral exploration activities. Under these regulations, mining companies must obtain ATI from the MRNF before conducting certain exploration activities that may impact the land, such as drilling, trenching, bulk sampling, and rock stripping. The ATI process involves (i) submitting detailed work plans, and (ii) engaging with local municipalities and Indigenous communities to gather and address their questions and comments. This collaborative approach aims to balance resource development with environmental stewardship and community interests. ​

2025 Exploration Program

Graphano is preparing to commence its exploration campaigns this quarter, focusing on trenching, targeted sampling, and drilling to further delineate graphite mineralization across its holdings. The Company remains dedicated to conducting its operations responsibly, with ongoing engagement with local communities and stakeholders.

About Graphano Energy

Graphano Energy Ltd. is an exploration and development company that is focused on evaluating, acquiring, and developing energy metals resources from exploration to production.

Graphite is one of the most in-demand technology minerals that is required for a green and sustainable world. The Company's Lac Aux Bouleaux property, situated adjacent to Canada's only producing graphite mine, in Quebec, Canada, has historically been an active area for natural graphite. With the demand for graphite growing in some of the most prominent and cutting-edge industries, such as lithium batteries in electric cars and other energy storage technologies, the Company is developing its project to meet the demands of the future.

ON BEHALF OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS

Luisa Moreno

Chief Executive Officer and Director

E: info@graphano.com

Neither TSX Venture Exchange nor its Regulation Services Provider (as that term is defined in the policies of the TSX Venture Exchange) accepts responsibility for the adequacy or accuracy of this release.

Cautionary Note Regarding Forward-Looking Statements:

This news release contains certain "forward-looking information" and "forward-looking statements" (collectively "forward-looking statements") within the meaning of applicable securities legislation. All statements, other than statements of historical fact, included herein, without limitation, are forward-looking statements. Forward-looking statements are frequently, but not always, identified by words such as "expects", "anticipates", "believes", "intends", "estimates", "potential", "possible", and similar expressions, or statements that events, conditions, or results "will", "may", "could", or "should" occur or be achieved. Forward-looking statements in this news release relate to, among other things, upcoming exploration campaigns aimed at further delineating graphite mineralization on the Company's properties, and ongoing engagement with communities and stakeholders. There can be no assurance that such statements will prove to be accurate and actual results and future events could differ materially from those anticipated in such statements. Such factors include, without limitation, risks associated with possible accidents and other risks associated with mineral exploration operations, the risk that the Company will encounter unanticipated geological factors, risks associated with the interpretation of exploration results, the possibility that the Company may not be able to secure permitting and other governmental clearances necessary to carry out the Company's exploration plans, the risk that the Company will not be able to raise sufficient funds to carry out its business plans, and the risk of political uncertainties and regulatory or legal changes that might interfere with the Company's business and prospects.
